title: Quantum Simulation of Gauge Theories for Particle and Nuclear Physics
abstract: Lattice field theory, along with its algorithmic and hardware ecosystems, has been at the forefront of computational particle and nuclear physics. It continues to deliver impressive results on the hadronic spectrum, structure, decays, and reactions. Yet, this vigorous campaign has fallen short in addressing a range of problems involving dense matter and general dynamical phenomena. The reason is that such problems require an exponential scaling of computing time and space in system size. Quantum simulation, enabled by quantum-computing algorithms and hardware technology, promises a way forward by offering several polynomially efficient algorithms compared with their inefficient classical counterparts. Lattice gauge theorists have engaged in a multi-pronged program to leverage such new possibilities, and have steadily advanced the state of theory, algorithm, and hardware implementations and co-design. In this talk, I motivate the quantum-computational lattice-field-theory program; introduce the questions such a program is expected to address and the strategies it involves; report on recent progress; and end with a note on challenges and opportunities ahead.
summary: This paper reviews the development of quantum simulation techniques for lattice gauge theories to overcome the limitations of classical Monte Carlo methods. It highlights the synergy between quantum algorithms, hardware co-design, and classical high-performance computing in studying the fundamental dynamics of particle and nuclear physics.
main_problem: Classical lattice field theory faces exponential scaling bottlenecks, such as the fermion sign problem and the inability to access real-time dynamics, when studying dense matter and non-equilibrium processes.
main_result: Quantum simulation offers a polynomial-efficiency alternative for studying gauge theories, with recent progress demonstrating successful simulations of string breaking, particle production, and quench dynamics on various quantum hardware.
method: A hybrid classical-quantum approach using digital (Trotterization, qubitization) and analog simulation techniques, integrated with classical HPC for state preparation and data analysis.
model_or_system: Lattice Gauge Theories (LGTs) including the Standard Model, QCD, QED, and simpler models like the Schwinger model, U(1), and SU(N) in (1+1)D and (2+1)D, implemented on platforms such as superconducting circuits, trapped ions, and Rydberg arrays.
key_observables: Hadronic spectrum, S-matrices, scattering amplitudes, parton distribution functions (PDFs), entanglement entropy, and real-time correlation functions.
important_parameters: Lattice spacing, finite volume, chemical potential, temperature, and the truncation of the Hilbert space.
main_assumptions: The necessity of truncating infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces for continuous gauge groups and the reliance on heuristic algorithms for state preparation due to the lack of proven quantum advantage in generic state preparation.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate a roadmap from theory to simulation, a workflow for hybrid quantum-classical computing, examples of non-equilibrium quench dynamics, and specific results like string breaking, PDFs, and phase diagrams.
paper_structure: The paper motivates the quantum-computational LGT program, identifies the limitations of classical Monte Carlo, details the algorithmic and hardware strategies (digital, analog, and hybrid), reports on recent experimental successes, and discusses future challenges and opportunities.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how quantum computing hardware (trapped ions, Rydberg arrays) can be used to simulate complex gauge dynamics and non-equilibrium phenomena that are classically intractable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A roadmap for leveraging quantum simulation and quantum computation in particle and nuclear physics.

Figure 2: Depicted are the quantum-simulation steps. Conventional lattice-QCD computations can inform and facilitate the state-preparation step, while high-performance (classical) computing will be needed to store and analyze vast quantum measurements. Time evolution can be accelerated by quantum processors, providing quantum advantage in many applications.
Figure 3: Examples of nonequilibrium quench dynamics in select (2+1)D LGTs: (a) is adopted from Ref. [47], (b) from Ref. [48], and (c) from Ref. [49].
Figure 4: Stages of thermalization dynamics in a (2+1)D 𝑍2 LGT learned from the nonequilibrium state’s entanglement spectrum. Plots are adopted from Ref. [51].
Figure 5: Examples of false-vacuum decay and string breaking in quench dynamics in select LGTs: (a) is adopted from Ref. [52], (b) from Ref. [53], (c) from Ref. [54], and (d) from Ref. [55].
